prediction.prompt: You privately think option A is best, but the three people before you all publicly chose B. Rationally, you might now:
options: Ignore your own signal and choose B too
prediction.reveal: Information cascades: once enough people act publicly, it becomes rational to ignore your own private evidence and copy them — and everyone after you inherits the same distortion. Try it below.

questions.prompt: An information cascade forms when people rationally:
options: Ignore others and use only private evidence
Discard their own signal and copy prior public actions
questions.explanation: Each person infers from others’ visible choices; past a point, public behaviour outweighs private information.

questions.prompt: A cascade can lock a whole crowd onto a wrong choice because early public actions:
options: Hide the private signals that follow
questions.explanation: Once people copy rather than reveal, later private signals never enter the public pool — the crowd stops learning.

questions.prompt: Cascades are fragile: a little new public information can:
options: Flip the entire crowd at once
questions.explanation: Because the cascade rests on thin information, a credible new signal can reverse everyone rapidly.

prediction.prompt: A risk that is easy to recall (a dramatic news story) tends to be judged as:
prediction.reveal: Availability cascades: a self-reinforcing loop where a vivid, repeated claim becomes more available in memory, feels truer, gets repeated more, and cascades through public discourse.

questions.prompt: An availability cascade is driven by:
options: Repetition making a claim feel more probable
questions.explanation: Ease of recall is mistaken for likelihood; repetition amplifies availability, which amplifies repetition.

questions.prompt: ‘Availability entrepreneurs’ accelerate the loop by:
options: Repeating and dramatising a claim to keep it salient
questions.explanation: Actors with an interest in the narrative feed the cycle, detaching perceived risk from actual risk.

questions.prompt: The best antidote to an availability cascade is usually:
questions.explanation: Grounding perception in frequencies counteracts the salience distortion.

prediction.prompt: A bank is solvent, but a rumour spreads that it may fail. If enough depositors withdraw ‘just in case’, the bank:
options: Can be driven to failure by the withdrawals themselves
prediction.reveal: Bank runs are self-fulfilling: fear of collapse rationally motivates withdrawal, and mass withdrawal causes the very collapse feared. A coordination trap.

questions.prompt: A bank run is self-fulfilling because withdrawing is rational when:
options: You expect others to withdraw first
questions.explanation: It is individually rational to run if you think others will — even a healthy bank can fail on illiquidity.

questions.prompt: Deposit insurance stops runs mainly by:
options: Removing the incentive to withdraw first
questions.explanation: If your deposit is safe regardless, the race to withdraw disappears — the coordination trap is defused.

questions.prompt: Bank runs illustrate that a system can fail from:
options: Beliefs and coordination, not just fundamentals
questions.explanation: Expectations can drive outcomes independently of the underlying balance sheet.

prediction.prompt: Deadly crowd crushes usually happen because people:
options: Panic and act irrationally as a mob
Are packed to a density where physical forces take over, regardless of intent
prediction.reveal: Crowd-crush dynamics are largely physical: above a critical density, shockwaves travel through bodies and individuals lose control of their own movement. Not moral panic — fluid-like mechanics.

questions.prompt: Fatal crowd crushes are best understood as:
options: Density-driven physical dynamics beyond individual control
questions.explanation: Once density crosses a threshold, propagating forces move people involuntarily — a fluid-like failure.

questions.prompt: The key control variable for crush safety is:
questions.explanation: Managing flow and density — not policing ‘panic’ — is what prevents crushes.

questions.prompt: Blaming victims for ‘panicking’ is misleading because:
options: At critical density no individual can control their motion
questions.explanation: The failure is systemic and physical; individual intent is largely irrelevant.

prediction.prompt: In 1637 a single tulip bulb sold for the price of a house. Manias like this are mainly powered by:
options: The belief that someone else will pay more (greater-fool dynamics)
prediction.reveal: Manias and delusions: prices detach from value when people buy expecting to resell to a ‘greater fool’. The bubble lasts only while new buyers keep arriving.

questions.prompt: A speculative mania is sustained by:
options: Expectations of selling to a greater fool
questions.explanation: Buyers accept absurd prices believing a later buyer will pay more — until the buyers run out.

questions.prompt: Manias collapse when:
options: The last greater fool has bought
questions.explanation: When the flow of new buyers dries up, the greater-fool chain breaks and prices crash.

questions.prompt: Charles Mackay’s lesson is that people ‘go mad in herds’ and recover:
questions.explanation: Collective delusion spreads fast but unwinds gradually as individuals regain judgment.

prediction.prompt: A moral panic (over witches, ‘video-game violence’, etc.) is characterised by public alarm that is:
prediction.reveal: Moral panics: a condition or group is defined as a threat to social values, amplified by media and ‘moral entrepreneurs’, with reaction far exceeding the real danger.

questions.prompt: A moral panic features reaction that is:
options: Disproportionate to the threat, with a folk-devil scapegoat
questions.explanation: Cohen’s model: media amplification and moral entrepreneurs create a ‘folk devil’ and outsized alarm.

questions.prompt: Media amplification contributes to moral panics by:
questions.explanation: Selective, dramatic coverage inflates perceived prevalence — close kin to the availability cascade.

questions.prompt: Moral panics tend to:
options: Subside once the folk devil loses novelty
questions.explanation: Panics have a life-cycle; attention fades and the reaction recedes.

prediction.prompt: People in masks, uniforms, or anonymous crowds often behave more extremely because anonymity:
prediction.reveal: Deindividuation: immersion in a group plus anonymity can reduce self-monitoring and personal accountability, lowering the threshold for behaviour one wouldn’t do alone.

questions.prompt: Deindividuation involves a loss of:
questions.explanation: Anonymity and group immersion weaken the internal brakes that normally guide behaviour.

questions.prompt: Modern accounts refine deindividuation as a shift toward:
options: Salient group norms over personal norms
questions.explanation: People don’t become mindless; they act on the local group’s norms rather than personal ones (the SIDE model).

questions.prompt: Adding identifiability (name badges, cameras) tends to:
questions.explanation: Re-individuating people reinstates the accountability anonymity had removed.

prediction.prompt: Sit among laughing people and you feel more amused; among anxious people, more tense. Emotions:
prediction.reveal: Emotional contagion: moods transfer through mimicry of expressions, posture and voice — often below awareness — synchronising a crowd’s feeling.

questions.prompt: Emotional contagion spreads largely through:
options: Automatic mimicry of expressions and tone
questions.explanation: We unconsciously copy others’ facial and vocal cues, then feel the matching emotion.

questions.prompt: A calm, steady leader can dampen a fearful crowd by:
options: Providing calm cues that others mimic
questions.explanation: Contagion runs both ways: composed signals can spread composure.

questions.prompt: Online, emotional contagion can occur:
options: Even through text and feeds, without direct contact
questions.explanation: Studies show mood can transfer through purely textual social-media exposure.

prediction.prompt: Everyone privately doubts the emperor’s new clothes, yet all praise them. This gap between private belief and public statement is:
options: Common and can persist for years
prediction.reveal: Preference falsification: people misrepresent their true preferences under social pressure, so public opinion can diverge massively from private opinion — and flip suddenly when the pressure breaks.

questions.prompt: Preference falsification means people publicly express:
options: A preference they don’t privately hold, due to social pressure
questions.explanation: Fear of sanction leads people to voice acceptable rather than actual views.

questions.prompt: Regimes can look stable then collapse overnight because falsification:
options: Hides real opinion until a trigger unmasks it
questions.explanation: Hidden opposition is invisible until a spark lets people reveal it — then preferences cascade (Kuran).

questions.prompt: Preference falsification makes public opinion:
options: A reliable guide to private belief
Potentially a poor guide to private belief
questions.explanation: Visible consensus may mask deep private disagreement.

prediction.prompt: Asked which line matches a reference, the answer is obvious. But if several confederates first give the same wrong answer, what fraction of people conform at least once?
prediction.reveal: Asch’s conformity experiments: about 75% of people gave a clearly wrong answer to match the group at least once. See how group size and a single ally change the pressure below.

questions.prompt: Asch showed that on an unambiguous task, group pressure could make people:
options: Publicly give an obviously wrong answer
questions.explanation: Roughly a third of critical trials produced conformity to a plainly incorrect majority.

questions.prompt: Adding one dissenting ally to the group:
questions.explanation: A single non-conforming partner breaks the unanimity that drives the pressure.

questions.prompt: In the simulator, conformity pressure rises with majority size but plateaus after about:
questions.explanation: Asch found the effect saturates around three to four unanimous confederates.

prediction.prompt: In a dark room, a stationary pinpoint of light appears to move (the autokinetic effect). Groups asked how far it moved will:
options: Converge on a shared ‘norm’ that persists
prediction.reveal: Sherif’s autokinetic studies: on genuinely ambiguous tasks, people build a shared group norm and then keep using it even alone — norms are internalised, not just performed.

questions.prompt: Sherif’s autokinetic study shows norms form fastest when the situation is:
questions.explanation: Uncertainty makes people rely on others to define reality — informational influence.

questions.prompt: Unlike Asch’s public compliance, Sherif’s norm was:
options: Internalised and used even when alone
questions.explanation: The shared estimate became each person’s private frame of reference.

questions.prompt: A norm seeded in an ambiguous group can:
questions.explanation: Replacement studies show arbitrary norms passing to newcomers long after the originators leave.

prediction.prompt: In Milgram’s study, what share of ordinary people administered what they believed were the maximum, dangerous shocks when an authority insisted?
prediction.reveal: Milgram’s obedience studies: roughly 65% went to the maximum voltage under authority. Read the chapter’s critique — the framing and ethics are heavily contested.

questions.prompt: Milgram’s core finding was that obedience to authority could override:
options: Personal conscience for many ordinary people
questions.explanation: A majority complied with an authority’s instructions to harm, against their own discomfort.

questions.prompt: Obedience dropped most when:
options: The authority was remote or peers rebelled
questions.explanation: Proximity of authority raised obedience; disobedient peers and distant authority lowered it.

questions.prompt: The chapter’s critique notes Milgram’s results are:
options: Contested on ethics, variability, and how participants understood the task
questions.explanation: Later scrutiny of archives and framing complicates the simple ‘we all obey’ reading — replication humility.

prediction.prompt: Cialdini distilled influence into six principles. Which pair most directly exploits collective behaviour?
prediction.reveal: Cialdini’s six principles — reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity — are levers of compliance. Social proof and scarcity most directly harness what others do and fear of missing out.

questions.prompt: Cialdini’s ‘social proof’ principle says we look to others’ behaviour especially when:
options: We are uncertain or the situation is ambiguous
questions.explanation: Uncertainty makes the crowd’s behaviour a shortcut for the ‘correct’ action.

questions.prompt: ‘Only 3 left in stock!’ leverages the principle of:
questions.explanation: Perceived scarcity raises desirability and urgency.

questions.prompt: Cialdini’s principles are powerful because they are:
options: Cognitive shortcuts that usually serve us but can be exploited
questions.explanation: They are heuristics — efficient by default, manipulable by design.

prediction.prompt: A restaurant with a queue outside attracts more customers than an empty one nearby, even without tasting either. This is:
options: Social proof — using others’ choices as evidence of quality
Bad luck for the empty one
prediction.reveal: Social proof: we treat others’ actions as information about what is correct or good, which is efficient — and exactly what fuels cascades and bubbles.

questions.prompt: Social proof is a heuristic that treats popularity as:
questions.explanation: ‘If many chose it, it’s probably good’ — usually helpful, sometimes badly misleading.

questions.prompt: Social proof can manufacture outcomes when displayed counts are:
questions.explanation: Fabricated popularity hijacks the heuristic — a bridge to Book Five’s astroturfing.

questions.prompt: Social proof links directly to information cascades because both rest on:
options: Inferring correctness from others’ visible choices
questions.explanation: They share the same engine: copying others’ actions as information.

prediction.prompt: Someone collapses on a busy street. Are they more likely to get help if many bystanders are present, or few?
prediction.reveal: The bystander effect: as the number of witnesses rises, each individual feels less personal responsibility, and help can become less likely. Diffusion of responsibility plus pluralistic ignorance.

questions.prompt: The bystander effect is driven partly by:
questions.explanation: ‘Someone else will act’ spreads across the group, lowering each person’s felt duty.

questions.prompt: A second driver of bystander inaction is pluralistic ignorance, where each person:
options: Reads others’ calm as evidence it isn’t serious
questions.explanation: Everyone looks calm because everyone is watching everyone else — and infers ‘no emergency’.

questions.prompt: Naming a specific person (‘you in the red coat, call an ambulance’) helps because it:
options: Reassigns personal responsibility to one individual
questions.explanation: Direct address defeats diffusion by making the duty unambiguous.

prediction.prompt: You go along with the group either because you want to be right, or because you want to be liked. These are:
options: Two distinct influences — informational and normative
prediction.reveal: Informational influence (others as evidence of reality) versus normative influence (others as a source of approval). Asch is mostly normative; Sherif mostly informational.

questions.prompt: Normative influence is conformity motivated by:
questions.explanation: You comply to belong, even when you privately disagree.

questions.prompt: Informational influence tends to produce:
questions.explanation: Treating others as evidence updates what you actually believe, not just what you say.

questions.prompt: Anonymity should reduce which influence most?
questions.explanation: Remove the audience and the need for approval (normative) falls; informational influence can persist.

prediction.prompt: In a classroom nobody understands the lecture, but no one asks a question because everyone looks calm. Each student concludes:
options: ‘I must be the only one lost’
prediction.reveal: Pluralistic ignorance: privately most people reject or doubt something, but each wrongly assumes they are alone because everyone else stays silent.

questions.prompt: Pluralistic ignorance is a mismatch between:
options: Private belief and the belief each person attributes to others
questions.explanation: A shared private view coexists with the false belief that ‘everyone else thinks otherwise’.

questions.prompt: Pluralistic ignorance can prop up an unpopular norm because:
options: Each person conforms to a majority that doesn’t actually exist
questions.explanation: People obey a phantom majority, sustaining norms almost no one privately holds.

questions.prompt: Publicly revealing true private opinions tends to:
questions.explanation: Once people learn others agree, the illusory majority evaporates.

prediction.prompt: A cohesive, high-status team under pressure to agree tends to make decisions that are:
options: Sometimes worse, as dissent is suppressed
prediction.reveal: Groupthink: the drive for unanimity in a cohesive group overrides realistic appraisal of alternatives — self-censorship, illusion of unanimity, and pressure on doubters.

questions.prompt: Groupthink is the tendency of cohesive groups to prioritise:
options: Consensus and harmony over critical evaluation
questions.explanation: Janis: the wish for agreement suppresses doubts and alternatives.

questions.prompt: A symptom of groupthink is:
options: Self-censorship and an illusion of unanimity
questions.explanation: Members silence doubts and mistake silence for agreement.

questions.prompt: A structural fix for groupthink is to:
options: Assign a devil’s advocate and gather independent views first
questions.explanation: Structured dissent and independent judgment restore the conditions for good collective decisions.
